h1Title: Angioimmunoblastic T-Cell Lymphoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma (AITL) is the second most common peripheral T-cell lymphoma, accounting for 15-20% of all T-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas worldwide and approximately 1-2% of all non-Hodgkin lymphomas. It arises from mature CD4-positive follicular helper T (TFH) cells within lymph node germinal centers and is defined by a characteristic combination of generalized lymphadenopathy, constitutional symptoms, polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia, and florid autoimmune features that often lead to misdiagnosis as an autoimmune or infectious disease before lymphoma is confirmed. Median age at diagnosis is 65-70 years. Overall survival at 5 years is approximately 30-40% with standard chemotherapy, though autologous stem-cell transplant in first remission improves outcomes in eligible patients. Novel agents targeting AITL-specific molecular vulnerabilities — IDH2, HDAC, EZH2 — are in active clinical trials.

keyStats.prevalence: AITL accounts for 15-20% of peripheral T-cell lymphomas (PTCL); overall incidence approximately 0.5-1 per 100,000 per year in Western countries; slightly higher incidence in Asian populations (up to 25% of PTCL)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects adults over 60 years; male:female ratio approximately 1.2:1; no strong racial predisposition but slightly higher frequency in Asian cohorts
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 60-70 years; rare under 40 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1,000-2,000 new cases per year in the United States; global incidence data limited by diagnostic heterogeneity of T-cell lymphomas

definition: Angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma (ICD-10: C86.5) — also called nodal follicular helper T-cell lymphoma, angioimmunoblastic type in the WHO 2022 classification — is a mature CD4-positive T-cell neoplasm arising from follicular helper T (TFH) cells, the specialized CD4 cells that normally reside within germinal centers and provide B-cell help. The WHO 2022 Classification of Lymphoid Tumors reclassified AITL under the umbrella of 'nodal T follicular helper (TFH) cell lymphoma' reflecting the shared cell of origin with other TFH-derived entities (follicular T-cell lymphoma, nodal PTCL with TFH phenotype). AITL is distinguished by a characteristic histology — paracortical expansion, prominent high endothelial venule (HEV) proliferation, branching follicular dendritic cell (FDC) meshworks, and abundant reactive CD20-positive B-cell infiltrates — combined with TFH immunophenotype (CD10, BCL6, CXCL13, PD-1 expression). Molecular hallmarks include frequent somatic mutations in DNMT3A, TET2, and IDH2 (the latter almost uniquely found in AITL, occurring in 20-30% of cases and defining a therapeutic target for enasidenib). RHOA G17V hotspot mutation is identified in 50-70% of AITL. EBV-infected B cells are almost universally present in the tumor microenvironment but are bystanders rather than the neoplastic cell. The disease is managed by hematologic oncologists and lymphoma specialists.

typesClassification.description: Most advanced form with complete effacement of lymph node architecture; florid HEV proliferation, expanded FDC meshworks, and dense mixed inflammatory infiltrate with the neoplastic TFH cells. Associated with the most pronounced systemic features: hypergammaglobulinemia, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and immune-complex deposition.

Early pattern with preservation of follicular architecture and hyperplastic germinal centers. May be initially diagnosed as reactive lymphadenopathy or another follicular lymphoma; TFH markers and molecular testing confirm AITL lineage.

Approximately 20-30% of AITL harbor an IDH2 R172 hotspot mutation, producing the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate that inhibits TET2 and DNMT3A-dependent epigenetic regulators. Defines a therapeutically targetable subgroup amenable to enasidenib (IDH2 inhibitor) in clinical trials.

typesClassification.type: AITL with EBV-associated large B-cell lymphoma transformation
typesClassification.description: In 5-15% of cases, the abundant EBV-infected bystander B cells undergo clonal expansion to produce a concurrent EBV-positive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. These patients require treatment directed at both the T-cell and B-cell components simultaneously.

primarySymptoms: Generalized lymphadenopathy — painless enlargement of multiple peripheral lymph node groups (cervical, axillary, inguinal) — present at diagnosis in virtually all patients.
B symptoms — drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss exceeding 10% over 6 months, and fever above 38.5°C — in over 70% of patients at diagnosis.
Skin rash — pruritic or generalized maculopapular eruption, often resembling a drug reaction, present in 30-50% of patients at diagnosis and frequently triggering the initial clinical evaluation.
Hepatosplenomegaly — enlargement of liver and spleen from lymphomatous infiltration and immune-mediated reaction, causing early satiety, left upper quadrant discomfort, and abdominal fullness.
Pleural effusion, ascites, or pericardial effusion from serosal involvement, occurring in 20-30% of patients.
Arthralgias or polyarthritis resembling rheumatoid arthritis, with joint pain and swelling in multiple small joints, from immune-complex deposition.
Fatigue and malaise, often severe, from the combination of constitutional disease, anemia, and hypergammaglobulinemia.
Edema of the face, legs, or arms from hypoalbuminemia (secondary to immune-mediated protein loss) and lymphatic obstruction.
Recurrent bacterial and opportunistic infections from functional immune deficiency despite the lymphoproliferative process — pneumococcal pneumonia, herpes zoster, and CMV reactivation are common.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent generalized lymphadenopathy in a patient over 60 years that fails to regress over 4-6 weeks and is associated with any systemic symptom — requires urgent biopsy rather than watchful waiting.

An unexplained drug-rash-like eruption in a patient who has not started any new medication, combined with lymphadenopathy — a classic AITL presentation that is frequently attributed to drug reaction for months.
Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia found incidentally on serum protein electrophoresis in a patient with constitutional symptoms — a common initial laboratory clue to AITL.
Direct antiglobulin test (Coombs) positive anemia with lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms — immune-mediated hemolytic anemia is an early laboratory feature in 10-20% of AITL patients.
emergencySigns: Rapidly progressive respiratory failure from bilateral pleural effusions or pulmonary infiltrates in a patient with known or suspected AITL — requires urgent admission, imaging, and thoracentesis.
Severe autoimmune hemolytic anemia with hemoglobin below 6 g/dL and hemodynamic instability — requires emergency transfusion, high-dose steroids, and accelerated oncological evaluation.

Septicemia from opportunistic organisms (Pneumocystis jirovecii, CMV, invasive fungi) in a severely immunocompromised patient — requires broad antimicrobial therapy and concurrent oncological management.

Hypercalcemia (corrected calcium above 3.5 mmol/L) from cytokine-driven bone resorption — causes altered consciousness, arrhythmia, and renal failure; requires IV hydration and bisphosphonates urgently.

causes.cause: Somatic mutations in TET2, DNMT3A, and RHOA in TFH progenitor cells
causes.description: The dominant molecular initiating events in AITL are early clonal somatic mutations in TET2 (epigenetic regulator; mutated in 70-80% of AITL) and DNMT3A (DNA methyltransferase; 20-30%) within hematopoietic progenitor cells. These 'pre-malignant' mutations are followed by RHOA G17V mutation in the committed TFH cell, producing the full malignant phenotype. RHOA G17V impairs normal Rho-GTPase signaling and promotes TFH cell survival.

IDH2 gain-of-function mutations produce the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG), which competitively inhibits TET2 and histone demethylases, creating widespread epigenetic dysregulation. IDH2 mutation is a nearly AITL-specific aberration within the T-cell lymphoma landscape and defines a druggable target for IDH2 inhibitors such as enasidenib.

causes.cause: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) reactivation in the tumor microenvironment
causes.description: EBV-infected B cells are present in almost all AITL tumors but are not the primary neoplastic cell. EBV reactivation in the AITL microenvironment — driven by impaired T-cell immune surveillance of EBV-infected B cells — contributes to B-cell activation, hypergammaglobulinemia, and systemic symptoms. In 5-15% of cases these B cells clonally expand to create a concurrent EBV-positive DLBCL.

causes.cause: Dysregulation of follicular helper T-cell differentiation
causes.description: TFH cells are the normal counterpart to AITL cells. Oncogenic AITL driver mutations force constitutive TFH differentiation, producing neoplastic cells that continue to express TFH markers (CXCL13, PD-1, ICOS, CD10, BCL6) and aberrantly promote germinal center formation, B-cell proliferation, follicular dendritic cell expansion, and angiogenesis in the lymph node.

The profound immune dysregulation in AITL — polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia, autoantibodies (direct Coombs, anti-smooth muscle, cold agglutinins), immune-complex deposition — stems from the aberrant B-cell help provided by malignant TFH cells. The autoimmune manifestations often dominate the early clinical picture, leading to initial diagnoses of systemic lupus, Sjogren syndrome, or vasculitis before lymphoma is recognized.

riskFactors.description: Median age at diagnosis is 60-70 years; accumulation of TET2, DNMT3A, and RHOA mutations in aging hematopoietic clones is the probable explanation. AITL is exceedingly rare under 40 years.

Pre-existing autoimmune conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, Sjogren syndrome, systemic lupus) are reported at higher-than-expected frequency in AITL patients. Chronic immune activation may promote TFH cell expansion and somatic mutation accumulation.

Methotrexate and other immunosuppressants used for rheumatoid arthritis or inflammatory bowel disease have been associated with increased risk of T-cell lymphoproliferative disorders, though causality versus underlying autoimmune disease as the shared risk factor is unclear.

TET2 and DNMT3A mutations in hematopoietic progenitors — the 'first hit' events in AITL — are also the commonest somatic mutations in CHIP (age-related clonal hematopoiesis in otherwise healthy older adults). CHIP carriers are at elevated risk of hematological malignancy including AITL.

Nearly all adults are EBV seropositive, but the EBV-rich microenvironment in AITL requires a specific host immune context — the combination of TFH cell-driven impaired EBV surveillance — rather than simply prior EBV infection.
Slight male predominance (ratio approximately 1.2:1) in most large series; the biological basis is not established but may reflect hormonal effects on TFH cell regulation.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 years represent the overwhelming majority of AITL cases; the median age at diagnosis in most series is 60-70 years.
Asian populations show a slightly higher proportion of AITL among all PTCL subtypes (up to 25%) compared with Western populations (15-20%), though the absolute incidence difference is modest.
Both sexes are affected with a slight male predominance; no strong racial or ethnic predisposition beyond the Asia-Pacific observation.
Patients with pre-existing autoimmune disease — rheumatoid arthritis, Sjogren syndrome, systemic lupus — may be at modestly elevated risk.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of AITL requires lymph node biopsy and typically proceeds in three stages: (1) histological review showing the characteristic AITL pattern (paracortical expansion, high endothelial venule proliferation, follicular dendritic cell meshwork expansion, mixed inflammatory infiltrate); (2) immunophenotyping by immunohistochemistry showing TFH markers (CD4+, CD10+, BCL6+, CXCL13+, PD-1+, ICOS+) on the neoplastic cells and EBV-positive large B cells in the background; and (3) molecular or genetic testing identifying RHOA G17V by allele-specific PCR or next-generation sequencing, and IDH2 R172 mutation. T-cell receptor gene rearrangement by PCR confirms clonality. Fine-needle aspiration is insufficient — core-needle or excisional biopsy of the most accessible lymph node is needed to preserve architecture. Once histological diagnosis is established, staging CT-PET of chest, abdomen, and pelvis, bone marrow trephine biopsy, and blood tests — including LDH, beta-2 microglobulin, albumin, complete blood count, serum protein electrophoresis, immunoglobulins, direct Coombs test, and uric acid — complete the workup. Autoimmune antibody screening (ANA, dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-smooth muscle, anti-cardiolipin) documents the immune phenotype and guides supportive management. The IPI (International Prognostic Index) and the AITL-specific PIT (Prognostic Index for T-cell lymphoma) score risk at diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Excisional or core-needle lymph node biopsy with immunohistochemistry
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides tissue for morphological diagnosis and comprehensive immunophenotyping with TFH markers (CD10, BCL6, CXCL13, PD-1, ICOS) and EBV-ISH for background B cells
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Core-needle biopsy under local anaesthesia (20-30 minutes); excisional biopsy under general anaesthesia where architecture preservation is needed. Full pathology results including immunohistochemistry and FISH in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Molecular testing: RHOA G17V, IDH2 R172, TET2, DNMT3A mutations by NGS or allele-specific PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms molecular hallmarks of AITL; IDH2 mutation specifically identifies patients eligible for IDH2 inhibitor trials
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on biopsy tissue in the molecular pathology lab; NGS panel results typically in 7-14 days. Increasingly incorporated into standard diagnostic workup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Stages disease across all sites and establishes baseline for response assessment; AITL shows moderately avid FDG uptake (typically SUVmax 5-15, lower than DLBCL)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Radiotracer injection 60 minutes before a 20-minute scan. Standard staging procedure prior to all lymphoma chemotherapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents stage IV disease; involved in 40-50% of AITL cases at diagnosis; detects the pre-malignant TET2/DNMT3A clonal hematopoiesis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small sample under local anaesthesia from the posterior iliac crest. Brief, dull-aching procedure. Results in 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum protein electrophoresis, immunoglobulins, and direct Coombs test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia — characteristic of AITL; Coombs test documents autoimmune hemolytic anemia present in 10-20% of patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia (broad elevation of IgG, IgA, or all Ig classes) is present in over 50% of AITL patients and is a diagnostic clue.

diagnosticTests.test: T-cell receptor (TCR) gene rearrangement by PCR or NGS
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms T-cell clonality; helps distinguish AITL from reactive T-cell lymphadenopathy, which can be histologically similar
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on biopsy tissue; results in 5-7 days. A clonal TCR rearrangement in the context of the TFH histology and immunophenotype confirms malignancy.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of AITL remains challenging; no curative chemotherapy regimen has been established in randomized controlled trials for this disease. The standard first-line approach is CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone) or the more intensive CHOEP (CHOP plus etoposide), based on its established use across peripheral T-cell lymphomas. Complete response rates to first-line CHOP/CHOEP are approximately 40-60%; however, relapse is common and 5-year OS is only 30-40%. Adding novel agents to CHOP is the focus of current clinical trials. Romidepsin (a histone deacetylase inhibitor) combined with CHOP improved PFS in the ACE-LY-302 trial; enasidenib (IDH2 inhibitor) showed activity in IDH2-mutated AITL in single-arm studies. For patients achieving first complete remission who are under 65-70 years and medically fit, consolidation with autologous stem-cell transplant (ASCT) is recommended based on phase II studies showing 5-year EFS of approximately 50-60% with this approach — a substantial improvement over chemotherapy alone. Allogeneic SCT produces potentially curative graft-versus-lymphoma effects but carries higher treatment-related mortality and is reserved for relapsed/refractory disease or high-risk patients without ASCT response. Azacitidine (a hypomethylating agent) and belinostat (HDAC inhibitor) have single-agent activity in relapsed AITL. Short-course high-dose steroids are used as a bridge to manage severe immune manifestations (autoimmune hemolytic anemia, pleural effusions) while awaiting chemotherapy.

medicalTreatments.name: CHOP (cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m², doxorubicin 50 mg/m², vincristine 1.4 mg/m², prednisone 100 mg/day d1-5) every 21 days for 6-8 cycles
medicalTreatments.description: Standard first-line chemotherapy for peripheral T-cell lymphomas including AITL. Administered every 3 weeks for 6-8 cycles. Etoposide is added (CHOEP) in patients under 60 years to improve response depth.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete response rate 40-60%; 5-year OS approximately 30-40% without consolidation SCT; better outcomes in younger patients with early-stage disease

medicalTreatments.name: Romidepsin (14 mg/m² days 1, 8, 15 of 28-day cycle) plus CHOP (Ro-CHOP)
medicalTreatments.description: HDAC inhibitor added to CHOP based on preclinical synergy and the phase III ACE-LY-302 trial. An exploratory TFH subgroup analysis of AITL showed improved PFS with Ro-CHOP versus CHOP alone. Approved in several jurisdictions for PTCL.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AITL subgroup of ACE-LY-302: median PFS 24 months (Ro-CHOP) vs 14 months (CHOP); overall trial result negative for unselected PTCL

medicalTreatments.name: Autologous stem-cell transplant (ASCT) in first complete remission
medicalTreatments.description: For eligible patients aged under 65-70 years achieving CR or near-CR after induction chemotherapy: BEAM or BCNU-based conditioning followed by autologous stem-cell rescue. Consolidates remission and extends survival.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase II series: 5-year EFS approximately 50-60% with ASCT vs approximately 25-30% without in matched comparisons; supported by NCCN and ESMO guidelines as standard of care in eligible patients

medicalTreatments.name: Azacitidine (75 mg/m² SC/IV days 1-7 of 28-day cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: Hypomethylating agent with activity against TET2- and DNMT3A-mutated AITL cells by restoring epigenetic regulation. Used as single-agent relapse therapy or in combination for unfit patients. Approved for relapsed/refractory AITL in some countries.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate approximately 25-30% in relapsed AITL; deeper responses in TET2-mutated cases; median duration of response 8-12 months

medicalTreatments.name: Belinostat (1,000 mg/m² days 1-5 of 21-day cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-generation HDAC inhibitor approved by the FDA (2014) for relapsed or refractory PTCL including AITL. Used as single-agent therapy in the post-transplant or chemotherapy-refractory setting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 26% in the BELIEF trial of relapsed/refractory PTCL; complete response in 10%; median duration of response 13.6 months (PMID 25907231)

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisone or methylprednisolone (high-dose bridge therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) are used as a bridge to definitive chemotherapy to control severe autoimmune manifestations — autoimmune hemolytic anemia, pleural effusions, or severe systemic illness — and to reduce constitutional symptoms temporarily. Not curative.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 60-80% of patients within days; without concurrent chemotherapy, response is transient (weeks)

surgicalOptions.name: Lymph node biopsy (diagnostic only)
surgicalOptions.description: Excisional lymph node biopsy provides the tissue required for definitive AITL diagnosis and molecular profiling. No therapeutic surgical options exist for AITL; the disease is systemic at presentation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic tissue adequate in >95% of excisional biopsies; core-needle adequate in approximately 80% for immunophenotyping and molecular studies

alternativeTreatments.name: Clinical trial enrollment (IDH2 inhibitors, PI3K delta inhibitors, anti-PD-1)
alternativeTreatments.description: Given the poor outcomes of standard therapy, enrollment in clinical trials evaluating enasidenib for IDH2-mutated AITL, umbralisib, and pembrolizumab is strongly recommended for all suitable patients, particularly at relapse.

whySeeSpecialist: AITL requires diagnosis and management at a center with a dedicated lymphoma program. The diagnostic complexity — distinguishing AITL from reactive lymphadenopathy, autoimmune disease, or other T-cell lymphomas — requires experienced hematopathologists with access to TFH immunohistochemistry markers and molecular testing. Treatment decisions involving ASCT and clinical trials for novel agents are only available at specialized centers.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek evaluation by a hematologic oncologist with experience in peripheral T-cell lymphomas at a comprehensive cancer center with bone marrow transplant capability. Molecular profiling (IDH2, RHOA) should be performed to identify trial eligibility. Given the autoimmune features, rheumatology co-management may be needed for patients with significant immune manifestations.
hospitalCriteria: Hematopathology laboratory with TFH immunohistochemistry (CXCL13, PD-1, ICOS, BCL6, CD10) and EBV-ISH capability
Molecular oncology laboratory for NGS including RHOA G17V and IDH2 R172
ASCT program for eligible patients in first remission
Clinical trial infrastructure for AITL-specific protocols
ICU access for management of septic complications and severe autoimmune hemolytic anemia
keyFacilities: Comprehensive cancer centers with dedicated lymphoma programs
Academic medical centers with hematopathology expertise in T-cell lymphomas
Clinical trial networks for rare lymphoma subtypes (ECOG-ACRIN, Alliance)
preventionStrategies: No established prevention strategies exist for AITL, as the underlying molecular events (TET2, DNMT3A, RHOA mutations) are somatic and stochastic.
Monitoring for clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) in at-risk older adults remains research-stage; no intervention is currently proven to reduce progression risk.
Minimize unnecessary immunosuppression duration and intensity in patients with autoimmune disease, as sustained immune dysregulation may contribute to T-cell lymphomagenesis.

For patients with known TET2/DNMT3A CHIP detected incidentally, close clinical surveillance by a hematologist is prudent given the elevated lymphoma risk, though no preventive treatment is established.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain vaccination status for pneumococcal and influenza vaccines before starting chemotherapy; live vaccines are contraindicated during treatment.
Follow a low-microbial diet during periods of neutropenia to reduce infection risk from food-borne pathogens.
Adhere to Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis (co-trimoxazole) throughout and for 3-6 months after chemotherapy, as AITL and its treatment produce profound T-cell immunodeficiency.
Disclose all autoimmune symptoms to both the oncologist and any rheumatologist involved in co-management — autoimmune flares can herald lymphoma relapse or a new therapy-related complication.
Enroll in a clinical trial if relapse occurs — novel therapies (IDH2 inhibitors, anti-PD-1, PI3K inhibitors) are only accessible through trials in most jurisdictions.
Avoid alcohol during chemotherapy, particularly during corticosteroid courses that already raise glucose — AITL treatment immunosuppression is additive.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) during and after chemotherapy to maintain lean body mass and support immune reconstitution
Well-hydrated state — 2-3 L/day — to protect kidneys during chemotherapy and reduce tumor lysis risk
Iron and folate supplementation to support recovery from autoimmune hemolytic anemia and chemotherapy-induced marrow suppression
Small frequent meals if corticosteroids cause gastric discomfort; calcium and vitamin D supplements to limit steroid-induced bone loss
avoid: Raw or undercooked foods during neutropenia — Salmonella, Campylobacter, and Listeria are serious risks with impaired T-cell immunity
Unpasteurized products (cheese, milk, juice) during immunosuppression
Alcohol — increases hepatotoxicity of chemotherapy and interacts with corticosteroids and methotrexate
High-purine foods (organ meats, anchovies) if uric acid is elevated, to reduce hyperuricemia risk

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise during and after treatment reduces fatigue and supports cardiovascular health compromised by anthracycline exposure. Vigorous exercise should be avoided during neutropenia and thrombocytopenia. After ASCT, exercise rehabilitation should be graduated over 3-6 months. Post-transplant patients should avoid contact sports and crowded areas during the period of maximal immunosuppression (first 100 days).
dailyManagement: Take temperature twice daily and report any fever above 38.0°C immediately — neutropenic fever is a medical emergency in AITL patients on chemotherapy.
Take all prescribed prophylactic medications (co-trimoxazole for PCP prophylaxis, antifungal, aciclovir) at the specified times; do not discontinue without oncologist guidance.
Inspect skin daily for new rashes — AITL-related skin eruptions, drug reactions, and infection can all produce rash and must be distinguished by the oncologist.
Monitor and record edema, breathlessness, and abdominal distension daily and report changes — these may indicate progressive pleural effusion or ascites.
Attend all outpatient blood count monitoring — cytopenia can develop between cycles and trigger need for growth factor or transfusion support.
Keep a written record of all medications, including over-the-counter supplements, as polypharmacy interactions are common in the elderly AITL patient population.
prognosis: AITL carries one of the worst prognoses of the common lymphoma subtypes. The 5-year overall survival is approximately 30-40% with standard CHOP-based chemotherapy in most prospective series. High-risk features — performance status 2 or above, elevated LDH, bone marrow involvement, extranodal disease at multiple sites, and low albumin — are captured by the Prognostic Index for T-cell lymphoma (PIT), where a score of 2 or more is associated with 5-year OS below 20%. Autologous SCT in first complete remission improves outcomes to approximately 50-60% 5-year EFS in selected younger, fit patients. Relapsed disease has a particularly poor prognosis: median OS after first relapse is approximately 5-7 months with conventional salvage chemotherapy. EBV-associated large B-cell lymphoma transformation further worsens prognosis. Novel agents — particularly enasidenib for IDH2-mutated disease and anti-CD30 or PI3K-delta inhibitors in development — represent the best near-term hope for improving survival outcomes in this disease. AITL is considered incurable in the majority of patients with current standard therapy, and enrollment in clinical trials is the highest priority at every stage of treatment.
recoveryTimeline: First CT-PET response assessment after 2-3 cycles of CHOP/CHOEP (approximately 6-9 weeks). Complete remission confirmation at end of induction (12-16 weeks). ASCT consolidation 4-8 weeks after end of chemotherapy in eligible patients. Post-ASCT immune reconstitution requires 6-12 months; PCP prophylaxis continues for at least 6 months. Relapse most commonly occurs within 12-18 months of completing treatment.
complications: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia — occurs in 10-20% of patients; can be life-threatening if severe; managed with steroids and by treating the underlying lymphoma.

Severe and recurrent opportunistic infections — the profound T-cell deficit in AITL predisposes to Pneumocystis pneumonia, CMV reactivation, VZV zoster, invasive aspergillosis, and bacterial sepsis, particularly during and after chemotherapy.
EBV-driven large B-cell lymphoma transformation — concurrent or emerging EBV-positive DLBCL in 5-15% of cases worsens prognosis and requires combined-modality treatment.
Secondary myelodysplastic syndrome or AML — from alkylating agent (cyclophosphamide) and topoisomerase II inhibitor (etoposide) exposure; risk approximately 2-5% at 10 years.
Pleural and pericardial effusions causing respiratory and cardiac compromise, particularly at diagnosis or relapse, requiring drainage and urgent lymphoma therapy.
Anthracycline-related cardiomyopathy — cumulative doxorubicin toxicity requires echocardiographic monitoring and cardioprotection with dexrazoxane in patients receiving high cumulative doses.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Research Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient education and support resources for all lymphoma types including peripheral T-cell lymphomas and AITL.

supportResources.name: T-Cell Lymphoma Forum (TCLF)
supportResources.description: Dedicated patient and professional education organization focused on peripheral T-cell and cutaneous T-cell lymphomas; hosts AITL-specific resources and clinical trial listings.

supportResources.name: Leukemia & Lymphoma Society
supportResources.description: Financial assistance, patient navigation, educational resources, and clinical trial matching services for lymphoma patients.

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute — T-Cell Lymphoma
supportResources.description: NCI PDQ evidence-based treatment summaries for T-cell NHL subtypes including AITL.

confusedWithConditions.name: Burkitt lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Burkitt lymphoma is a high-grade B-cell lymphoma (CD20+, BCL2-, 100% Ki-67, MYC translocation) with an extreme proliferative rate and very high curability. AITL is a mature T-cell lymphoma (CD3+, CD4+, TFH phenotype) with more indolent but less curable behavior, autoimmune features, and a different treatment strategy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AITL is one of the lymphomas most frequently misdiagnosed as SLE because it produces antinuclear antibodies, immune-complex deposition, polyarthritis, and rash. SLE affects younger women and lacks the generalized lymphadenopathy of AITL. Lymph node biopsy resolves the diagnosis; SLE biopsies show reactive changes, not the clonal TFH infiltrate of AITL.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcoidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcoidosis produces generalized lymphadenopathy, constitutional symptoms, and elevated serum ACE — overlapping with AITL presentations. Sarcoid biopsies show non-caseating granulomas without clonal T-cell proliferation. AITL biopsies show paracortical expansion with TFH cell clusters and high-endothelial venule proliferation, confirmed by TFH immunomarkers.

relatedConditions.name: Burkitt lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are aggressive lymphomas managed by hematologic oncologists; Burkitt is the B-cell counterpart requiring different chemotherapy with higher cure rates; both are treated at specialized lymphoma centers.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: AITL is a subtype of peripheral T-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma; general lymphoma management principles (staging, chemotherapy, SCT) apply with AITL-specific modifications.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: AITL frequently mimics SLE with autoantibodies, polyarthritis, and immune complex disease; both require specialist care and are on the differential for young-to-middle-aged adults with constitutional symptoms and lymphadenopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcoidosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Sarcoidosis is a frequent initial misdiagnosis for AITL due to overlapping features of lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms; biopsy distinguishes granulomatous sarcoid from the clonal T-cell infiltrate of AITL.

faqs.question: What is angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma (AITL)?
faqs.answer: AITL is an aggressive lymphoma arising from mature follicular helper T cells in lymph node germinal centers. It accounts for 15-20% of all T-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas. It typically presents in adults over 60 with generalized lymphadenopathy, constitutional symptoms, skin rash, and prominent autoimmune features such as hemolytic anemia and hypergammaglobulinemia.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of AITL?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms include generalized lymphadenopathy, B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), itchy skin rash, hepatosplenomegaly, and fatigue. Autoimmune features — joint pain, anemia, and pleural effusions — are common and often lead to initial misdiagnosis as lupus, drug reaction, or sarcoidosis.

faqs.question: Is AITL curable?
faqs.answer: AITL is not reliably curable with standard chemotherapy. The 5-year overall survival is approximately 30-40% with CHOP-based treatment. Autologous stem-cell transplant in first complete remission improves 5-year survival to approximately 50-60% in eligible patients under 65-70 years. Relapsed AITL has a median survival of only 5-7 months.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for AITL?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is CHOP or CHOEP chemotherapy (6-8 cycles). Romidepsin is added in some regimens for TFH-subtype lymphomas. Eligible patients in first remission proceed to autologous stem-cell transplant to reduce relapse risk. Relapsed disease is treated with belinostat, romidepsin, azacitidine, or clinical trials.

faqs.question: Why is AITL often mistaken for lupus or autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: AITL produces polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia, autoantibodies (antinuclear, anti-smooth muscle, cold agglutinins), immune complex deposition, polyarthritis, and rash — all features of systemic autoimmune diseases. The misdiagnosis is common and delays lymphoma treatment by months in many cases. Lymph node biopsy is required to distinguish them.

faqs.question: What genetic mutations are found in AITL?
faqs.answer: AITL is characterized by somatic mutations in TET2 (70-80%), DNMT3A (20-30%), RHOA G17V (50-70%), and IDH2 R172 (20-30%). The TET2 and DNMT3A mutations arise early in hematopoietic progenitors; the RHOA mutation occurs later in the committed TFH cell. IDH2 mutation defines a targetable subgroup for IDH2 inhibitors.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for AITL?
faqs.answer: The 5-year overall survival is approximately 30-40% with standard therapy; among high-risk patients (PIT score 2-4), it falls below 20%. Autologous SCT in first remission improves outcomes in fit patients under 70. Relapse has a poor prognosis with median survival of 5-7 months on standard salvage. Clinical trials offer the best hope for improved outcomes.

faqs.question: What is the role of EBV in AITL?
faqs.answer: EBV-infected B cells are present in almost all AITL tumors but are not the neoplastic cell — they are bystanders that contribute to hypergammaglobulinemia and systemic symptoms. In 5-15% of cases these EBV-positive B cells clonally expand to produce a concurrent diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, worsening the overall prognosis.

faqs.question: Who gets AITL?
faqs.answer: AITL predominantly affects adults over 60 years; the median age at diagnosis is 60-70 years. Both sexes are affected with a slight male predominance. It is slightly more common among Asian populations. Pre-existing autoimmune disease and age-related clonal hematopoiesis (TET2/DNMT3A mutations) are the main identified risk factors.

faqs.question: How is AITL diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires excisional or core-needle lymph node biopsy showing the characteristic histology (TFH cell infiltrate with high endothelial venules), TFH immunophenotype (CD4+, CD10+, CXCL13+, PD-1+), EBV-positive B cells, and T-cell clonality. Molecular testing for RHOA G17V and IDH2 R172 is increasingly standard.

faqs.question: What is a follicular helper T cell and why does it matter in AITL?
faqs.answer: Follicular helper T cells (TFH) are specialized CD4 cells in germinal centers that provide help for B-cell antibody production. In AITL, the neoplastic cells are malignant TFH cells that continue to express TFH markers (CXCL13, PD-1, BCL6) and aberrantly drive B-cell activation, explaining the hypergammaglobulinemia and autoimmune features.

faqs.question: Is autologous stem-cell transplant used for AITL?
faqs.answer: Yes. Autologous SCT is recommended for eligible patients under 65-70 years who achieve first complete remission. Phase II studies show approximately 50-60% 5-year event-free survival after ASCT in first remission, compared with 25-30% with chemotherapy alone. It is considered the standard of care for fit patients in NCCN and ESMO guidelines.

faqs.question: What does AITL skin rash look like?
faqs.answer: The AITL skin rash is typically a widespread, itchy, maculopapular eruption affecting the trunk and extremities that resembles a drug reaction or viral exanthem. It is present in 30-50% of patients at diagnosis. It often leads to months of investigation for allergy or drug hypersensitivity before lymphadenopathy leads to biopsy.

faqs.question: Are there targeted therapies for AITL?
faqs.answer: Yes, in development. Enasidenib (an IDH2 inhibitor) showed activity in IDH2-mutated AITL in early-phase trials. Romidepsin (HDAC inhibitor) is approved for relapsed PTCL. Azacitidine targets TET2-mutated disease. Anti-PD-1 antibodies (pembrolizumab) are in trials. All patients with AITL should be evaluated for clinical trial eligibility.

faqs.question: Can AITL come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, relapse is common — over 60% of patients relapse within 12-18 months of completing first-line chemotherapy. Autologous SCT reduces the relapse rate in first remission. Relapsed AITL has a poor prognosis and should be managed with novel agents in clinical trials where possible, or with belinostat or romidepsin approved for relapsed PTCL.

simpleName: AITL (angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma, nodal TFH lymphoma)
metaDescription: Angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma (AITL): aggressive TFH lymphoma mimicking autoimmune disease. CHOP chemotherapy, transplant and prognosis reviewed.
